includedSources.name: The Dark Side of the Boom

items.title: Strait of Hormuz will not return to normal — IRGC controls the valve
items.idea: The MOU gives Iran effective control of the Strait of Hormuz. IRGC has stopped issuing new transit permits; current outbound traffic is only existing permit holders. VLCC inflows into the strait are now dominantly Iran-bound with minimal leakage. HFIR is publishing daily tanker count tables distinguishing sanctioned vs unsanctioned vessels by type.
items.angle: Anyone modeling a return to pre-war Hormuz traffic is wrong on the data. The structural oil supply constraint has been formalized. Keeps refiner crack spread risk alive. Long $VLO $MPC on structural refiner margin support.
items.tweetA: The market is pricing Hormuz as a temporary disruption. The data says otherwise. IRGC has stopped issuing new permits. VLCC inflows into the strait are almost exclusively Iran-bound. Anyone modeling a return to normal is making a serious analytical error.
items.tweetB: Forget the peace talks. Watch the tanker data. Since the MOU, crude tankers entering Hormuz are flowing to Iran with minimal leakage elsewhere. The supply route is no longer neutral — it's Iranian-controlled. Crack spreads should be structurally elevated. $VLO $MPC $PSX

charts.desc: Windward AI tanker traffic table — vessel inflows into Hormuz by type/sanction status

items.title: HFIR thesis: Hormuz is binary — no in-between scenario exists
items.idea: HFIR's framework: either the US restarts conflict and wins (restoring normal Hormuz) or Iranian control is permanent. The 'partial reopening' scenario the market is pricing doesn't exist. Daily tanker count tracking has begun to prove this empirically.
items.angle: Market base case of partial normalization may be categorically wrong. The entire energy supply curve needs re-anchoring to Iranian cooperation as a permanent structural condition.
items.tweetA: HFIR's Hormuz call: this is binary. Either the US restarts and wins — restoring normal transit. Or Iranian control is permanent. The market is pricing some in-between scenario that doesn't exist. The tanker data is choosing door #2.
items.tweetB: "Anyone assuming the Strait of Hormuz will return to normal is making a serious analytical mistake." — HFIR. They now publish daily tanker counts to prove it. The data will become obvious within days. $USO $CL_F

charts.desc: Daily tanker count tables (sanctioned/unsanctioned by vessel type) — new recurring publication

items.title: Warsh FOMC debut: the real regime change is at the Fed, not in Tehran
items.idea: Chair Warsh's first FOMC meeting sent one unambiguous message: price stability is back at the center. Persistently high prices described as 'a threat to the durability of the expansion.' CME FedWatch now pricing 70% odds of September rate hike. The Fed put framework that anchored risk assets through 2024-25 may have expired.
items.angle: The Warsh framework changes the asymmetry of the Fed reaction function. Bad data no longer delays hikes — it accelerates them. Re-price the vol surface. Short 2-year bonds.
items.tweetA: The real regime change isn't in Tehran. It's in Washington. Warsh's FOMC debut was unambiguous: price stability is back at the center, no dovish escape hatch. The market spent two years buying every dip under Powell. That playbook may now be expired.
items.tweetB: Warsh didn't just sound hawkish. He redefined what the Fed is optimizing for. 'Persistently high prices are a threat to the durability of the expansion.' That's a mandate statement, not a data-dependent hedge. 70% September hike odds. Re-price the vol surface.

items.title: AI is no longer a universal productivity story — it's a scale, access, and political-power story
items.idea: Noshad Shah at Citadel Securities: the early AI thesis was everyone benefits from productivity gains. The emerging reality is that AI concentrates at whoever controls the infrastructure, access, and political relationships. This shift has direct investment implications for who wins in the AI stack.
items.angle: Two different investment frameworks: 'AI lifts all boats' vs 'AI consolidates power at the infrastructure layer.' The second framework is winning. Explains Microsoft's move against OpenAI/Anthropic and global regulatory backlash.
items.tweetA: Citadel's Noshad Shah: AI is no longer a universal productivity story. It's a scale, access, and political-power story. That distinction changes every AI investment thesis. The infrastructure bottleneck isn't compute anymore — it's control.
items.tweetB: The early AI framework: everyone gets more productive. The emerging framework: AI concentrates at whoever controls the infrastructure, the data pipeline, and the political relationships. Microsoft vs OpenAI/Anthropic is the first major battle inside this second framework.

items.title: Iran deal removed war premium — NOT the geopolitical leverage premium on oil
items.idea: The MOU removed kinetic-escalation risk. It did not remove the geopolitical leverage premium from Iran having permanent control of Hormuz access as a political tool. Two different premia. The market conflated them in the selloff.
items.angle: Crude is pricing like the problem is solved. The leverage option remains in Iranian hands and will be exercised politically, not militarily. $75 oil with Iran controlling transit permits is still cheap on a normalized basis.
items.tweetA: The Iran deal removed the war premium from oil. It did NOT remove the geopolitical leverage premium. These are different things. $75 crude with Iran controlling Hormuz access as a political tool is still cheap — one renegotiation failure away from $95.
items.tweetB: Markets collapsed the Hormuz risk premium after the MOU. But two risks existed: kinetic escalation (priced out) and geopolitical leverage (still fully intact). Iran now has a permanent option to restrict access. That option has value. The market isn't pricing it.

items.title: Cerebras packaging yield ~20%: fix it from 20% to 50% and GMs go from 42% to Nvidia-level 72%
items.idea: Near-100% wafer yield from TSMC but ~20% packaging yield is the only explanation for Cerebras' 42% gross margins. WSE-3 rack ASP revised to $700-850K (from $300K), inference speed at 0.7K tok/s/user (not 3K). If packaging yield improves from 20% to 50%, GMs jump to ~72% (Nvidia-level). Entire CBRS investment thesis lives or dies on a manufacturing process problem.
items.angle: Credible packaging yield improvement roadmap = CBRS deeply undervalued. No roadmap = economics never work at scale. Should be the only question on the next investor call.
items.tweetA: Cerebras has ~100% wafer yield and ~20% packaging yield. That's the only model that fits the financial numbers. Improve packaging from 20% to 50% and gross margins go from 42% to Nvidia-level 72%. The entire $CBRS thesis is a manufacturing process problem, not a chip design problem.
items.tweetB: Irrational Analysis model on $CBRS: ASP ~$700-850K per rack, inference ~0.7K tok/s/user (not 3K). Hardware GMs at 42% only make sense at ~20% packaging yield. Every point of yield improvement is worth 1.5+ GMs. The investor day should be entirely about the yield roadmap.

charts.desc: Excel model for COGS/GM and inference tokenomics (linked in post)

items.title: KV cache competing with model weights in SRAM is Cerebras' core bottleneck
items.idea: KV cache and model weights compete for the same SRAM on WSE. As context windows grow, KV cache crowds out model capacity. Solution is off-chip KV to NAND — but WSE-3 lacks I/O bandwidth for streaming. Two uncorrelated sources confirm 'face-to-face' I/O is in development but involves extreme packaging challenges. Until solved, WSE economics break with large models and long contexts.
items.angle: The I/O architecture tells you whether WSE-4 opens the large-model/long-context market. If solved, TAM opens massively. If not, stays in fast-inference/small-model niche.
items.tweetA: Cerebras' core bottleneck: KV cache competes with model weights for the same SRAM. You can't run large models at long contexts efficiently because context eats parameter space. Off-chip to NAND is the fix — but WSE-3 lacks the I/O bandwidth. Until WSE-4 solves this, TAM is capped. $CBRS
items.tweetB: "ADMIT THE PROBLEM AND GO FIX IT. YOU RAISED MONEY FROM THE IPO GO SPEND IT TO FIX THE DISASTER BOTTLENECK." Irrational Analysis on Cerebras KV cache vs SRAM trade-off. The CEO dodged the question on how many CS-3 were used for Kimi AI. The analysts noticed. $CBRS

items.title: WSE-4 floating-point format support is the single most important variable for Cerebras
items.idea: WSE-3 lacks FP8 support — critical gap as all competitors support FP8 or lower. Three decisive variables for WSE-4: FP8/FP4 format support; on/off-wafer I/O to fix KV cache offload; packaging yield improvement roadmap. Without FP8 on WSE-4, architecture can't compete at scale.
items.angle: WSE-4 spec reveal is the next major binary event for $CBRS. FP8 + improved I/O = rerating catalyst. FP16 stay = significant bear case.
items.tweetA: The only three things that matter for Cerebras WSE-4: (1) FP8/FP4 format support — staying FP16 would be existential. (2) Off-wafer I/O to fix KV cache bottleneck. (3) Packaging yield improvement roadmap. Everything else is noise. $CBRS
items.tweetB: WSE-3 is from 2024. WSE-4 design is presumably far along. If it stays FP16 in a world where every competitor supports FP8 or lower, the architecture falls behind permanently. FP8 is now table stakes. The spec reveal is the trade event for $CBRS.

items.title: Nadella publicly breaks with OpenAI and Anthropic — Microsoft weaponizes DeepSeek
items.idea: Nadella delivered public critique of frontier AI concentration and announced Microsoft weighing hosting DeepSeek on Copilot. Microsoft released low-cost models and Copilot Cowork (multi-model agent). After Copilot subscribers migrated to Gemini in H2 2025, Microsoft has no competitive frontier model — so it's now using capital to commoditize the frontier model layer. OpenAI and Anthropic IPOs coming later this year.
items.angle: Most significant intra-AI ecosystem split yet. Microsoft's largest AI partners are now competitive targets. $MSFT is effectively short the frontier AI incumbents ahead of their IPOs.
items.tweetA: Nadella just weaponized DeepSeek against his own partners. Microsoft — OpenAI and Anthropic's biggest backer — is weighing hosting the Chinese model both companies accused of copying their work. When your investor starts selling your competitor's product, that's a signal. $MSFT
items.tweetB: Microsoft trailed in frontier AI (users migrated to Gemini in H2 2025). Nadella's response: don't compete on models, commoditize the model layer. Host DeepSeek, release cheap Copilot models. OpenAI and Anthropic IPOs are coming this year into this dynamic.

items.title: USD/JPY at 161.95 — policy-risk trade, Katayama-Bessent call changes the math
items.idea: USD/JPY back near July 2024 intervention level but now a policy-risk trade not a yield trade. Katayama-Bessent call key signal: Tokyo alone = bilateral defense; Bessent showing up = broader warning on dollar strength. Real risk is not August 2024 tsunami but a sharp tactical repricing of Washington's tolerance for a stronger dollar.
items.angle: August 2024 everyone learned. That doesn't make 162 safe — it makes the squeeze more targeted and faster. If Bessent shows up, the stop-loss cascade from short-yen crowd is violent. 4-5 JPY downside move can happen in a session.
items.tweetA: USD/JPY at 162 is not a yield trade anymore. It's a policy-risk trade. The Katayama-Bessent call is the signal: Japan acting alone says Tokyo is unhappy. Bessent showing up says Washington may be uncomfortable with dollar direction. That distinction is worth 4-5 JPY in a session.
items.tweetB: Korea's AI equity wobble is usually how August 2024 started: equities crack first, vol rises, leverage gets expensive, THEN the funding currency bites. The sequence is starting again. The market thinks it's immune because it remembers 2024. That's exactly what makes it dangerous.

items.title: SK Hynix 'shift to general-purpose DRAM' + double-leveraged ETFs: 13.2% becomes 26.4%
items.idea: SK Hynix announced 'shifting focus to general-purpose DRAM' — suggesting AI memory tailwind may be maturing. Stock fell 13.2%. Korean regulators publicly regretted allowing double-leveraged single-stock ETFs (CSOP SK Hynix in HK), which translated the drop into a 26.4% loss for ETF holders. SOX remains more overbought than at any time since the dot-com peak even after the selloff.
items.angle: Leverage structure in AI semis has become extreme and self-amplifying. Double-leveraged single-stock ETFs in momentum names are the 2026 equivalent of portfolio insurance in 1987.
items.tweetA: Korea's regulator publicly regretted allowing double-leveraged single-stock ETFs after SK Hynix fell 13.2% — which became a 26.4% loss for ETF holders. When the product works exactly as designed and the regulator is horrified, that tells you something about where leverage is in the AI semi complex.
items.tweetB: After Tuesday's chip selloff, the SOX is STILL more overbought than at any time since the peak of the dot-com boom. This wasn't the correction. This was the warm-up. SK Hynix 'shifting to general-purpose DRAM' was the pin, not the balloon.

charts.desc: SOX vs 200dma overbought vs dot-com comparison; SK Hynix leveraged ETF AUM explosion; Korea market YTD

items.title: SaaS direct lending: $8B in 2015 → $538B in 2025 — all exposed to AI disruption + high rates
items.idea: Apollo's Torsten Slok: S&P 1500 Software Index's 30% decline in 8 months is AI disruption + rates higher-for-longer hitting simultaneously. Direct lending to SaaS exploded from $8B in 2015 to $538B in 2025. That capital is exposed to both forces compounding.
items.angle: $538B in leveraged SaaS exposure facing AI disruption is a credit event building. The credit market hasn't priced the dispersion between AI-native and legacy SaaS on leveraged debt.
items.tweetA: $538B in direct lending to SaaS companies (from $8B in 2015). That capital now faces two simultaneous threats: AI disrupting the business models it funded, and rates higher-for-longer squeezing debt service. $538B exposed to both forces compounding. $BXSL $ARCC $ORCC
items.tweetB: The SaaSpocalypse math: S&P 1500 Software Index -30% in 8 months. Apollo's Slok: it's AI disruption PLUS rate stress, not either/or. Every SaaS company on leveraged debt facing an AI competitor is in a double squeeze. Credit markets still pricing this as sector rotation, not credit cycle.

charts.desc: S&P Software vs SOX relative (8-month collapse); SaaS direct lending growth 2015-2025

items.title: BofA raises chip demand to $2.7T by 2030 (28% CAGR) — Micron EPS forecast +645% in 12 months
items.idea: BofA raised US chip demand forecast to $2.7T by 2030 from $2.3T, implying 28% annual growth 2025-2030. Driven by memory chip demand and AI data-center hardware. Micron's forecast Q3 EPS rose 645% in 12 months. Memory chip shortages and price inflation remain 'critical moving pieces' even after selloff.
items.angle: 28% CAGR in chip demand is the fundamental case even as market de-rates on valuation. Micron earnings tonight are binary: clear = selloff was a buying opportunity; miss = 2000-style multiple compression resumes.
items.tweetA: BofA revised chip demand to $2.7T by 2030 — 28% annual growth through the end of the decade. Micron's forecast EPS is up 645% in 12 months. Tonight's report is the first real earnings test of whether the selloff was overblown or early warning. $MU $NVDA
items.tweetB: After the SK Hynix selloff: SOX still dot-com-level overbought, BofA raises chip demand to $2.7T by 2030, Micron EPS forecast +645% in 12 months. Two things can be true: the fundamental AI chip cycle is real AND the market is priced beyond perfection. Tonight tells us which tension is winning. $MU

charts.desc: Micron EPS consensus estimates over 12 months — 645% upward revision

items.title: India: MSCI weight 20% → 12% while economy grew 7.7% — the biggest Hormuz collateral damage
items.idea: India's MSCI weight collapsed from 20% at 2024 peak to under 12%. Economy grew 7.7% FY2026 (7.8% Q4, above RBI and market expectations). Foreign investors sold MORE Indian equity in first 5 months of 2026 than all of 2025. Barclays' Rajadhyaksha calls it a compelling opportunity. Early signs of inflows returning per SocGen.
items.angle: India is the classic innocent bystander re-entry trade. Strong macro, extreme positioning, cause of selloff (Hormuz) moving toward resolution. If Hormuz reopens meaningfully, India re-rates fastest among EM.
items.tweetA: India's MSCI weight: 20% in 2024 → under 12% today. Economy grew 7.7% in fiscal 2026. Foreign investors sold more Indian equity in the first 5 months of 2026 than in all of 2025. A fundamentals-positioning disconnect of this magnitude does not come often. $EPI $INDA
items.tweetB: Barclays' Rajadhyaksha on India: 'A disconnect of this magnitude between fundamentals and positioning does not come along often.' India was the Hormuz conflict's biggest collateral damage victim. Now the war is ending and India's the obvious rebound trade.

charts.desc: India MSCI weight vs world; relative valuation collapse; SocGen inflow signals

items.title: D.E. Shaw: productivity growth above 5% in AI-exposed industries — double the economy-wide pace
items.idea: D.E. Shaw data: productivity growth has risen above 5% in industries most exposed to AI, including software — roughly double the economy-wide pace. If real and sustained, potentially deflationary, changing the Fed's calculus. Software companies may be seeing the productivity gains before the revenue disruption fully hits.
items.angle: 5% productivity growth in AI-exposed industries is potentially the most important datapoint for the macro regime. AI deflationary + Warsh tightening into it = policy error in slow motion. Long duration bonds thesis building.
items.tweetA: D.E. Shaw data: industries most exposed to AI are showing 5%+ productivity growth — double the economy-wide pace. If this is real and sustained, AI is deflationary. And the Warsh Fed tightening into AI-driven disinflation might be the next great policy error.
items.tweetB: The bears say AI is killing SaaS revenue. D.E. Shaw says AI-exposed industries are at 5%+ productivity growth, 2x the economy. These can both be true: software companies lose revenue to AI while becoming more productive with fewer people. The P&L converges — question is which effect wins on the timeline.

items.title: Goldman GSSZSPCE basket: own the Asian factory floor, not the rocket
items.idea: Goldman introduced Asia Space Economy Basket (GSSZSPCE) spanning launch/propulsion, satellite manufacturing, ground infrastructure, space-grade electronics. The thesis: post-SpaceX, the lasting returns accrue to the factory floor — Asian manufacturers, electronics firms, satellite suppliers. Under-owned, valuations below global peers. Sovereign programs + satellite deployment + AI infrastructure bottleneck support multi-year industrial cycle.
items.angle: Picks-and-shovels trade for the space economy — Asian version. Under-owned, cheaper than Western peers, sovereign spend as tailwind. Space economy doesn't need SpaceX valuations if you own the component suppliers.
items.tweetA: Goldman built a basket (GSSZSPCE) to own the Asian factory floor behind the space economy — satellite components, launch propulsion, space-grade electronics, ground infra. Not the rocket. The factories that supply every rocket. Under-owned, valuations below global peers, sovereign programs as tailwind.
items.tweetB: The first wave of the space trade chased SpaceX. The second wave may chase the Korean, Japanese, and Taiwanese firms making every antenna, component, and chip that goes into orbit. Goldman's Asia Space Economy basket is the '2015 rare earth trade' for the orbital buildout.

items.title: Dollar at 1-year-plus high + 70% September hike odds — beating every non-dollar asset including gold
items.idea: US dollar at fresh 1-year-plus high. CME FedWatch ~70% September rate hike odds. 10Y near 4.50%, 2Y at Feb 2025 levels. Gold slipped to ~$4,126. Iran deal, Hormuz mechanism, 60-day oil license — none offsetting dollar breakout. Thursday PCE is the first real test of whether lower oil softens September hike odds.
items.angle: When dollar is beating gold during geopolitical uncertainty, monetary regime signal is overwhelming geopolitical signal. September hike probability is the single variable to watch.
items.tweetA: US dollar at a fresh 1-year-plus high. 70% September hike odds on CME. 10Y near 4.50%. Gold at $4,126 and slipping. The Iran deal, the Hormuz mechanism, the 60-day oil license — none of it matters right now. The dollar is beating every non-dollar asset. This is the Warsh regime.
items.tweetB: When gold is falling during a Middle East conflict because the dollar is too strong, you're not in a geopolitical risk environment anymore. You're in a Warsh Fed environment. Thursday's PCE is the next pivot point. Hot print = September hike confirmed = dollar goes higher.

items.title: $717M crypto liquidations — BTC at $62K pressing $60K support; break opens $54K target
items.idea: BTC fell 2.5-3.4% to ~$62K, pressing critical $60,000 support. ETH hardest-hit major (-4.7-6.8% to ~$1,650). SOL -5.3%, BNB -3.4%, XRP -3.15%, HYPE -8.9%. ~$717M total liquidations. Friday ~$10B crypto options settlement with vol looking cheap. $60,000 binary: hold = range intact; break = $54K target; $59,130 cycle low is last defense.
items.angle: Macro-driven flush inside established range. $59,130 stop defines the trade. Thursday PCE is the catalyst: cool print reverses dollar headwind; hot print likely sends BTC to test $54K.
items.tweetA: BTC at $62K, pressing $60K support. $717M in crypto liquidations. ETH -5-7% because the most-loved names get sold hardest in a high-beta flush. The $60,000 line is binary: hold it and the range survives; break it and $54,000 opens. This is a dollar story, not a crypto story. $BTC $ETH
items.tweetB: $10B in crypto options settle Friday. Volatility is cheap going into it. BTC at $62K with $60K as the last major support. PCE Thursday is the catalyst that decides whether the Fed hike repricing reverses. High vol + binary catalyst + binary support = interesting setup either way. $BTC

charts.desc: BTC/ETH price charts with support levels and liquidation data

items.title: CLARITY Act (July 4) + MiCA + $8B XRP ETF flows: regulatory tailwinds building under macro selloff
items.idea: Ripple won preliminary MiCA approval from Luxembourg. CLARITY Act on Senate floor, White House July 4 signing target. Standard Chartered: $8B in XRP ETF flows if CLARITY passes. Russia's 'unfriendly crypto' bill targets USDC and BNB effective July 1. Global crypto regulatory bifurcation accelerating.
items.angle: Regulatory tailwinds (CLARITY, MiCA) are real and building — but dollar is overwhelming them short-term. XRP at $1.10 must hold. CLARITY/MiCA is the cleanest dated catalyst in crypto for July.
items.tweetA: Ripple won preliminary MiCA approval in Luxembourg. CLARITY Act targeting July 4 signing. Standard Chartered: $8B in XRP ETF flows if it passes. The regulatory tailwinds are real. The macro is currently drowning them out. When the dollar turns, the snap could be violent. $XRP
items.tweetB: Russia's 'unfriendly crypto' bill targets USDC and BNB effective July 1. EU's MiCA keeps expanding. US CLARITY Act targeting July 4. The global crypto regulatory bifurcation is accelerating. Geography is becoming a genuine risk factor for on-chain holdings. $BNB specifically carries a Russia-specific overhang.

items.title: Morgan Stanley spot SOL+ETH S-1s; Moody's on-chain; Ondo >430 tokenized assets
items.idea: Morgan Stanley filed amended S-1s for spot Solana (MSOL, 0.14% fee, staking) and Ethereum (MSSE, staking) trusts on NYSE Arca. Moody's brought credit ratings on-chain to Solana. Baillie Gifford's BAGEY tokenized bond fund live on Solana/ETH with BNY custody. Ondo >430 tokenized assets. Backpack tokenized SpaceX token crossed 10,000 holders. Institutional infrastructure built during the selloff.
items.angle: TradFi plumbing going into crypto is irreversible. Morgan Stanley + Moody's + Baillie Gifford in one day is institutional legitimization at unprecedented scale. Defines the structural floor.
items.tweetA: Morgan Stanley filed for spot Solana AND Ethereum trusts (with staking). Moody's put credit ratings on-chain on Solana. Baillie Gifford launched a tokenized bond fund on Solana with BNY custody. All in one day. The institutional plumbing got built during the selloff. $SOL $ETH
items.tweetB: Moody's credit ratings on Solana blockchain is underappreciated. Not because ratings are sacred — but because it signals that institutional capital will flow through on-chain infrastructure with formal risk assessments attached. This is how RWA tokenization scales from 430 assets to 430,000. $SOL

items.title: The 40-year stock-bond hedge is fracturing — inflation uncertainty breaks 60/40
items.idea: Capital Flows Research: the stock-bond hedge that anchored portfolios for 40 years is now fracturing. Core mechanism: inflation uncertainty flips the correlation regime. When inflation is high and uncertain, stocks and bonds fall together — the hedge disappears. Explains why S&P, Nasdaq, and Nikkei can all sell off on the same day. 60/40 was built on a negative stock-bond correlation that required low, stable inflation.
items.angle: If 60/40 is broken, forced rebalancing flows no longer cushion equity selloffs. This amplifies drawdowns and makes the next bear market more violent. Real assets, commodities, and inflation-benefiting strategies become the new diversifiers.
items.tweetA: The 40-year stock-bond hedge is fracturing. The mechanism: inflation uncertainty flips the correlation. In a Warsh Fed world with persistent inflation risk, bonds fall WITH stocks. 60/40 worked for 40 years under low, stable inflation. That era may be over. The rebalancing flows no longer cushion you.
items.tweetB: Why can S&P, Nasdaq, AND Nikkei all sell off on the same day? Because the stock-bond correlation regime has flipped. Inflation uncertainty makes bonds fail as a hedge when equities fall. Every risk parity fund, every 60/40 portfolio is running on an assumption that may no longer hold.

charts.desc: Stock-bond correlation regime chart; 60/40 return attribution; inflation vs correlation scatter

items.title: Korea rubber-band rebound: Goldman desk says institutions selling into retail dip-buying
items.idea: Korea's Kospi rebounded sharply after Tuesday's AI memory selloff — rubber-band effect. Goldman desk colour: foreign funds, local managers, hedge funds using the bounce to reduce exposure while retail buys the dip. Market moved from 'momentum trade' to 'earnings-validation trade.' Key test: Micron earnings + Samsung preliminary Q2 in early July.
items.angle: Distribution playbook: institutions sell into retail enthusiasm. Price recovers while bid quality deteriorates. Korea stays 'for sale on rallies' unless Micron/Samsung earnings validate the AI memory narrative.
items.tweetA: Goldman desk on Korea's rebound: foreign funds and hedge funds are selling strength into retail dip-buying. Price is recovering; conviction isn't. The rubber band snapped back — but the people holding the heavy end are still looking for the door. $MU earnings tonight is the test.
items.tweetB: Korea moved from a momentum trade to an earnings-validation trade in 48 hours. Micron tonight, Samsung Q2 in early July. If earnings are strong, institutional supply gets absorbed. If they disappoint, the distribution structure means the second leg down is worse than the first. $MU $005930 $000660

items.title: Only 17% of S&P 500 stocks beat the index — extreme concentration; Nasdaq-100 adds AI infra names
items.idea: Only 17% of S&P 500 stocks outperformed the index last month. Nasdaq-100 rebalance added Astera Labs, CoreWeave, Nebius, Rocket Lab, Teradyne; Marvell joins S&P 500. Per Schwab, broadening requires permanent Hormuz reopening to change the energy/inflation equation. Until then, passive rebalancing keeps flowing into same 15-20 AI infrastructure names.
items.angle: Index concentration is self-reinforcing through passive flows. Every Nasdaq-100 addition of CoreWeave/Astera Labs creates mandatory buying from $500B+ in index funds. The AI infra names that enter indices get the forced bid.
items.tweetA: 17% of S&P 500 stocks beat the index last month. Nasdaq-100 just added Astera Labs, CoreWeave, Nebius, Rocket Lab, Teradyne. Marvell enters S&P 500. Every passive rebalancing flows more money to the same 15-20 AI infra names. The index is becoming an AI infrastructure bet. $ALAB $CRWV
items.tweetB: The broadening thesis requires a permanent Hormuz reopening per Schwab — to change the energy/inflation equation keeping the Fed hawkish. Until then, the concentration story doesn't end. The AI infra names entering indices get the forced bid. Everything else stays crowded out.

items.title: Strategy's STRC preferred at $88.79 — $300M cash + BTC rebound couldn't push it to $100 par
items.idea: STRC closed at $88.79, bounced off record low $82.53. Despite $300M cash boost (total $1.4B), BTC rebound to $67K last week, and shift to bi-monthly dividends, STRC hasn't returned to $100 par. Theories: short selling, rotation to Strive's SATA preferred, leverage unwind. Strategy holds 846,842 BTC at ~$75,700 avg cost. Two main numbers to watch identified in premium section.
items.angle: When preferred stock can't hold par against a rising BTC backdrop, market is questioning the leverage architecture — not just the Bitcoin price. STRC is the stress sensor for the Strategy model. Watch it as BTC approaches the $60K test.
items.tweetA: Strategy's STRC preferred: $88.79 vs $100 par. Record low $82.53. $300M cash injection + BTC at $67K last week couldn't fix it. When the preferred of the world's largest corporate Bitcoin holder can't hold par on a BTC rally, the market is questioning the leverage structure. $MSTR $STRC
items.tweetB: STRC at $88 while MSTR holds 846K BTC at $75,700 avg cost. When bi-monthly dividends AND $300M cash AND a BTC rebound all fail to move STRC to par, something structural is being repriced. The $60K BTC test will clarify it. $MSTR

charts.desc: STRC daily candle chart showing drawdown from $100 par to $82.53 low

items.title: AV sensor tech (lidar, radar) spilling into eldercare, airports, robotics — $160B software market by 2035
items.idea: AV sensor technologies are finding applications beyond transportation: traffic management, airport operations, eldercare monitoring, infrastructure security, robotics. Despite slower-than-expected AV adoption, technology is diffusing broadly into adjacent markets. McKinsey projects AV software/electronics market at ~$160B by 2035. Competitive advantage shifting from vehicle engineering to AI infrastructure (data, compute, semiconductors, cloud).
items.angle: Standard technology diffusion pattern: technology built for one market deploys across many. The AV 'failure' story may be wrong about the winners — they're the sensor and AI stack suppliers, not the car companies.
items.tweetA: The AV 'failure' narrative misses the point. Radar, lidar, and sensing systems built for self-driving are now flowing into eldercare monitoring, airport ops, infrastructure security, and robotics. Technology developed for one market, deployed across many. McKinsey sees $160B in AV software by 2035.
items.tweetB: McKinsey: competitive advantage in autonomous driving is shifting from vehicle engineering to AI infrastructure — data, compute, semiconductors, cloud. Sounds familiar. Every major technology wave ends with the infrastructure layer capturing the value. AV is following the PC-to-cloud playbook.
